name: Rights & Legal Basics (US)
sourceAttribution: Compiled from ACLU 'Know Your Rights' guides, FEMA public guidance, USPS identification references, and state-bar public legal-aid materials. NOT legal advice. US-centric for v1. Laws vary by state and country; consult a licensed attorney for any specific situation. If in doubt, request a lawyer and stay silent.
license: CC-BY-4.0 (compilation); source citations carry their own public-domain or CC license

guides.title: Important — read this first
guides.content:
This pack is general information, US-centric, for emergency use. It is NOT legal advice and cannot substitute for a licensed attorney who knows your specific situation, jurisdiction, and the current state of law.

Laws vary widely:
• By country
• By state and even by city
• By time — laws change; cases get decided
• By individual circumstance — what's optimal for one person can be wrong for another

For any consequential interaction with authorities, the universal safe defaults are:
1. Request a lawyer.
2. Stay silent.
3. Do not consent to searches; do not resist physically.

The guides that follow give general framing — they exist for when comms are down and you can't reach a lawyer immediately. Reach a lawyer as soon as you can.

guides.title: If you are stopped or detained (US)
guides.sourceRef: ACLU 'Know Your Rights — Stopped by Police' guide (public CC-licensed), retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
NOT LEGAL ADVICE. General US framework only.

Key rights (US):
• You have the right to remain silent. To use it, you must say so out loud: "I am exercising my right to remain silent."
• You have the right to refuse consent to a search of your person, car, or home ("I do not consent to a search"). This does not mean officers won't search anyway — but your statement protects your later legal position.
• If you are detained, you have the right to ask if you are free to leave. If yes, calmly leave. If no, you are being detained.
• Once detained or arrested, you have the right to a lawyer. Say: "I want a lawyer" — clearly, repeatedly if questioning continues.

You may be required to identify yourself in some states ("stop and identify" laws). Beyond your name, you typically don't have to answer further questions.

Do NOT physically resist, even if you believe the stop is unjust. Document, don't litigate on the street. If you're injured or rights are violated, write down everything you remember as soon as you can — badge numbers, time, location, witnesses.

This is the absolute minimum. ACLU's full guides cover specific scenarios (immigration enforcement, protests, traffic stops) and are worth reading in calm times.

guides.title: Recording authorities in public (US)
guides.sourceRef: ACLU + First Amendment legal-aid public materials, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
NOT LEGAL ADVICE.

In the US, you generally have a First Amendment right to record police and other public officials performing their public duties in public spaces. This is well-established but enforcement varies and officers sometimes attempt to stop recording anyway.

Practical guidelines:
• Keep your phone visible. Don't conceal it.
• Stand a reasonable distance away — close enough to record, far enough not to interfere.
• Don't physically interfere with what officers are doing.
• If asked to step back, comply.
• If your phone is seized, you do NOT have to provide your passcode. Say verbally: "I do not consent to a search of this device."
• Police generally need a warrant to search the contents of a phone.

Audio recording is more complicated for private conversations:
• "One-party consent" states (most): you can record any conversation you're part of.
• "All-party consent" states (roughly a dozen — CA, FL, WA, MA, IL, PA, MI, MD, MT, NH, CT and others, sometimes only in certain contexts): all parties must consent to record private conversations. Public spaces where there's no reasonable expectation of privacy generally don't require consent. Verify your state before relying on this.

Know your state's law before relying on this. ACLU's state-by-state guides are the authoritative starting point.

guides.title: FEMA disaster assistance (US)
guides.sourceRef: FEMA — Apply for Assistance at DisasterAssistance.gov, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
After a federally declared disaster, individuals and households can apply for FEMA Individual Assistance. Covers things insurance does not — temporary housing, essential home repairs, certain personal property losses, replacement of disaster-related lost essentials, transportation.

How to apply:
• Online: DisasterAssistance.gov
• Phone: 1-800-621-3362 (TTY 1-800-462-7585)
• In-person: Disaster Recovery Centers (locations at FEMA.gov after declaration)

What you'll need:
• Social Security number
• Address of the damaged property
• Description of damage
• Insurance information
• Bank account info for direct deposit

Apply as soon as possible after the disaster declaration. There's a deadline — typically 60 days after declaration, often extended for major disasters. Check DisasterAssistance.gov for the current deadline for your specific declaration.

FEMA does NOT duplicate insurance — file an insurance claim first. FEMA fills gaps where insurance doesn't cover or pay enough.

If denied, you can appeal within 60 days. Many initial denials are reversed on appeal with better documentation.

guides.title: Insurance documentation before and after a disaster
guides.sourceRef: FEMA + state insurance commissioner public guides, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
NOT LEGAL ADVICE.

BEFORE a disaster:
• Photograph or video every room — pan slowly, open closets and cabinets, capture serial numbers on electronics.
• Save the inventory off-site: cloud storage AND a copy with a trusted relative in a different region.
• Keep policy numbers, agent contact, and insurer's claims line in your go-bag and stored digitally.
• Know your deductibles and what's covered. Standard homeowners DOES NOT cover floods (separate NFIP policy) or earthquakes (separate rider).

AFTER a disaster:
• Document damage with photos/video BEFORE you clean up. Insurer needs to see what was lost.
• Keep ALL receipts — temporary lodging, emergency repairs, replacement essentials. Itemize.
• Report the claim promptly. There are deadlines.
• Do NOT sign permanent repair contracts without verifying the contractor's license, insurance, and references. Disaster zones attract scammers.
• Don't accept the first settlement offer if it seems low. You have appeal rights.

Keep a paper notebook of every conversation: who you spoke with, when, what was said. Disaster claims can drag on for months.

guides.title: Identifying yourself when your phone is dead
guides.sourceRef: USPS + DHS REAL ID public guides, retrieved 2026-05-26.
guides.content:
NOT LEGAL ADVICE.

Phones fail in disasters. People without ID on their person can be turned away from shelters, denied prescription refills, or unable to prove relationships to dependents.

Keep PHYSICAL copies of:
• Government-issued photo ID (driver's license, passport, state ID)
• Health insurance card
• Critical prescription list (medication, dose, prescriber)
• Emergency contact list, including out-of-state contact
• A small recent photo of each family member (for re-identification if separated)
• Vaccination records for children and pets (some shelters require)

Store in a waterproof bag in your go-bag. Update once a year.

If you're in a state with REAL ID requirements, your driver's license should be REAL ID compliant. Federal facilities (and post-2025 commercial flights) require it. Look for a star in the corner of your license.

For children too young to remember addresses or phone numbers: a small laminated card with name, address, parents' names, and an out-of-state emergency contact, tucked into a pocket or backpack.
